body:
### Motivation.


vLLM-Omni runs one request across multiple stages (comprehension / generation / audio / image / latent), multiple replicas, and several modalities. Each stage has different KV usage and pressure. But every existing KV cache metric — upstream-native or already merged into omni — is a single engine-level total, with no stage / replica / modality dimension. With only that one total, these questions can't be answered:

1. Which stage holds the most KV?
2. Within one stage, which replica has the lowest occupancy and the most tail waste?
3. How much does KV usage differ across modalities (text / audio / image / latent)?
4. When latency gets worse, is it compute, scheduling, or block waste eating effective capacity?
5. After a change to prefix caching / chunking / routing / block size, how does CI catch a per-stage efficiency regression before merge?

On the Omni path, upstream metrics aren't just "low-dimensional" — they can outright fail. On the `OmniARScheduler` path, several upstream counters (`prefix_cache_*`, `prompt_tokens_total`, `kv_cache_usage_perc`) read 0 under real load. The cause is a 1-second throttle in `OmniSchedulerMixin.make_stats()`: within 1 second of the last stats it returns `None` and skips upstream's `super().make_stats()`, which is exactly where upstream reports prefix / token counts. Under the multi-stage scheduling cadence this stats layer is frequently swallowed.

This RFC's metrics flow through the orchestrator's per-request message bus, which bypasses that throttle, so they keep emitting on the Omni path even where the upstream counters above read 0. That blind spot, plus the missing attribution dimensions, is what this RFC addresses.

### Proposed Change.


Add a vLLM-Omni KV efficiency metrics module: a set of metrics labeled by stage / replica / modality, read directly from the KV allocator. It does not change allocator behavior, and lands across a few PRs.

All evidence below was measured on a real GPU (single RTX 5090, vLLM 0.23.0) using **Qwen3-TTS-12Hz-1.7B**, a genuine two-stage Qwen-family omni pipeline (talker → code2wav). Allocator reads come from `kv_cache_manager.get_block_ids()` (`source="kv_cache_manager"`); all numbers are independently recomputed from raw data.

### The metric family

All metrics share one label set: `model_name, stage, replica, modality, source`.

| Metric | Type | Meaning | Where it comes from |
|---|---|---|---|
| `vllm_omni:kv_footprint_tokens` | Gauge | KV token slots allocated in the stage's latest snapshot | Directly measured (`len(block_ids)·block_size`) |
| `vllm_omni:kv_footprint_bytes` | Gauge | estimated KV bytes | Computed (token count × bytes per token) |
| `vllm_omni:kv_block_occupancy_ratio` | Histogram | useful tokens / allocated slots | Computed (ratio of two ground-truth values) |
| `vllm_omni:kv_tail_waste_tokens` | Histogram | allocated slots − useful tokens | Computed |
| `vllm_omni:kv_fragmentation_ratio` | Histogram | tail waste / allocated slots |

comments.author: hsliuustc0106
comments.body: can you try to use a model which vllm-omni supports: Qwen3-Omni, Qwen3-tts or diffusion models as a good example to demonstrate the problem? Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct will be served with vllm alone.

comments.author: Ronnie-Rui
hsliuustc0106
comments.body:
Thanks for updating the evidence to use Qwen3-TTS.

Could you clarify the intended scope for the initial implementation? Should the first PR series cover only LLM/AR scheduler stages, or is the goal to cover all omni stage types from the start, including diffusion/audio/code2wav/generation stages?

This matters for the metric contract because allocator-backed KV footprint/occupancy can be defined fairly directly for LLM stages, while non-LLM or fallback sources may need separate semantics and should probably be labeled explicitly as estimates.

comments.author: Ronnie-Rui
comments.body:
> Thanks for updating the evidence to use Qwen3-TTS.
> 
> Could you clarify the intended scope for the initial implementation? Should the first PR series cover only LLM/AR scheduler stages, or is the goal to cover all omni stage types from the start, including diffusion/audio/code2wav/generation stages?
> 
> This matters for the metric contract because allocator-backed KV footprint/occupancy can be defined fairly directly for LLM stages, while non-LLM or fallback sources may need separate semantics and should probably be labeled explicitly as estimates.

Good question. Quick breakdown of your list:

- generation / code2wav — can be covered in the first series. Both are LLM stages with a real kv_cache_manager, so occupancy/footprint can be read straight from the allocator.
- diffusion — I'd leave out of the first series. It doesn't use paged KV/block ids at all, so occupancy/fragmentation don't map onto it — needs its own metric semantics, better as a follow-up.

On the estimate point: that's what the source label is for — allocator reads are source="kv_cache_manager", fallbacks are source="stage_tokens", so estimates are never silently mixed into ground-truth numbers.

So the first series would cover everything with a paged KV allocator (all LLM stages); diffusion later. Sound good?

comments.author: Ronnie-Rui
comments.body: @hsliuustc0106 Just checking whether the proposed initial scope sounds good. If so, could you please assign this to me? Thanks!

comments.author: hsliuustc0106
comments.body:
> [@hsliuustc0106](https://github.com/hsliuustc0106) Just checking whether the proposed initial scope sounds good. If so, could you please assign this to me? Thanks!

we can focus on LLM_AR/LLM_Generation stage type first. For diffusion, we have not implemented a general abstraction which should be ready in Q3.

comments.author: LHXuuu
comments.body:
I’m not fully convinced yet that these metrics are needed in the default Prometheus surface.

After #3576, upstream `vllm:*` metrics already carry `{stage, replica}` labels in vLLM-Omni, including the existing KV/scheduler/prefix-cache metrics. For production monitoring, that already lets us identify which stage/replica has KV pressure.

The proposed slot/tail-waste metrics seem to measure allocator-internal page overhead. Since paged KV cache allocates by block, unused slots in the last block are expected by design and bounded by block size. I’m not sure exposing that as `fragmentation`/`tail_waste` is actionable for users, unless there is evidence that this overhead causes real capacity/latency regressions that existing `kv_cache_usage_perc`, scheduler, and prefix-cache metrics cannot reveal.

Could you clarify the concrete operational decision these new metrics would enable? For example:
- What issue can be diagnosed only with `kv_tail_waste_tokens` / `kv_fragmentation_ratio`?
- Is this expected block-size overhead, or a real allocator inefficiency?
- Do we have a case where upstream KV usage with `{stage, replica}` looks healthy but these metrics catch a regression?
- Should these be debug/profiling metrics rather than default production Prometheus metrics?

I’m supportive if there is a clear regression/debugging case, but otherwise I think the existing KV usage metrics may already cover the main production monitoring need.

comments.author: Ronnie-Rui
comments.body:
Thanks @LHXuuu, that makes sense and I agree.
﻿
After #3576, the existing `vllm:*` metrics with `{stage, replica}` already cover the main need for spotting KV pressure by stage/replica. The proposed `tail_waste` / `fragmentation` are mostly expected paged-KV block rounding overhead, and I don't have a concrete case that `kv_cache_usage_perc` / scheduler / prefix-cache metrics can't already reveal. I won't push these as default Prometheus metrics for now.

title: [RFC]: vLLM-Omni KV Cache Efficiency and Attribution Metrics
